claim: Provider configuration in YOLO demand evidence dispatch has explicit safeguards to preserve tool capability boundaries and prevent cross-provider model leakage through executionConfig contract normalization.

evidence.summary: executionConfig explicitly clears Claude tool configuration flags (settings, claude_tools, claude_allowed_tools, claude_disallowed_tools) and sets claude_no_session_persistence to true for evidence agents
evidence.why: Independent verification of the core safeguard that prevents inherited tool restrictions in evidence mode.

evidence.summary: Tool profile normalization maps research and full profiles to normalized values and applies them to ai.agent_tool_profile
evidence.why: Confirms tool profiles are controlled and normalized rather than inheriting arbitrary provider settings.

evidence.summary: Codex provider normalization clears inherited Claude model references by checking ai.model for Claude strings and resetting ai.model
evidence.why: Direct evidence of cross-provider model configuration isolation preventing Codex from inheriting Claude models.

evidence.summary: buildProviderInvocation for Claude defaults to permission-mode and only sets tool flags when explicitly configured via ai.settings
evidence.why: Confirms the provider adapter design avoids hard-coded tool restrictions for Claude agents.

evidence.summary: Codex provider capabilities including model_selection, file_write, and shell_exec are based on sandbox mode, independent of Claude configuration
evidence.why: Shows provider-specific capabilities are isolated and do not leak between providers.

evidence.summary: normalizeAgentProvider maps provider aliases (anthropic, openai, shell, local) to canonical names (claude, codex, custom)
evidence.why: Independent verification that provider normalization prevents ambiguous references that could cause configuration drift.

evidence.summary: execution_policy defines boundary with project_mutation forbidden and allowed_write_roots as only the output artifact directory
evidence.why: Confirms the formal boundary specification that evidence agents must not modify project files.

evidence.summary: Test claude dispatch does not pass tool restriction flags and relies on boundary enforcement verifies settings and tool flags are empty strings
evidence.why: Test evidence confirming actual runtime behavior matches design contract.

evidence.summary: Test codex dispatch does not inherit a claude default model or force read-only sandbox verifies Codex gets clean config
evidence.why: Direct test evidence that cross-provider model configuration isolation is enforced.

evidence.summary: Test boundary blocks project file mutation even when agent results pass shows boundary snapshot detects violations
evidence.why: Confirms the boundary enforcement mechanism is active and blocks mutations regardless of agent success.

evidence.summary: Test claude full tool profile requires explicit authorization for full semantic mode verifies full profile requires allowFullAgentTools flag
evidence.why: Shows the system has guardrails preventing unintended tool profile downgrades.

assumptions: The dirty working tree state does not affect the evidence-dispatch.ts implementation under verification
The executionConfig normalization in lines 447-448 checking for Claude model strings in ai.model applies to all cases where Codex is selected after a Claude default
risks: If executionConfig normalization is bypassed or modified, provider-specific assumptions could leak into evidence agent configuration
The dirty working tree indicates uncommitted changes that may diverge from the tested behavior documented in the test suite

result.notes: Independent verification confirms all 12 evidence claims from the explorer. The executionConfig function has explicit normalization for Claude (lines 450-463) that clears tool configuration, and specific Codex model isolation (lines 447-448) that clears inherited Claude models. Cross-provider isolation is confirmed by both code and tests. The safeguards are in place as documented, but the dirty working tree should be committed before real agent stress testing to ensure tested behavior matches runtime state.
